moveAnnotations.annotation: The starting move of the Jobava London System. We're claiming the center and preparing to develop our pieces aggressively. Naroditsky chooses d4 because the Jobava setup creates immediate tactical threats while avoiding the massive theory of mainline Queen's Gambit or Nimzo-Indian positions.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 starts the Jobava London, claiming the center and creating immediate tactical threats while avoiding heavy theory.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black's most natural response, fighting for central control. By playing d5, Black mirrors our central pawn and prevents an immediate e4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d5 fights for central control and blocks e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: the defining move of the Jobava London! Instead of the traditional London move Nf3, we develop the knight to c3 where it pressures d5 and prepares the Nb5 jump targeting c7. The knight on c3 also supports a future e4 pawn break.
plans: Coordinate with Bf4 to control dark squares
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Jobava London: Nc3 pressures d5, prepares Nb5 targeting c7, and supports e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its ideal square, defending d5 and preparing to castle. From here, the game branches into many critical variations depending on Black's next moves.
alternatives: Bf5 — develops the bishop but allows the f3-g4 bishop hunt
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight defends d5 and prepares to castle, branching into critical variations.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Completing the Jobava London setup! The bishop comes to f4 BEFORE playing e3, keeping it active outside the pawn chain. Together, Nc3 and Bf4 create a powerful duo — the knight threatens Nb5 hitting c7, while the bishop already eyes that same square.
plans: e3 followed by Bd3 and O-O
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 before e3 keeps the bishop active outside the pawn chain, and with Nc3, both pieces target c7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black chooses the solid e6 setup, bolstering d5. However, this move blocks in the light-squared bishop and does nothing to prevent our Nb5 jump!
alternatives: g6 — leads to the sharp h4 attack
Bf5 — allows the f3-g4 bishop hunt
c6 — Slav setup, solid but passive
a6 — prevents Nb5 but wastes tempo
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e6 bolsters d5 but blocks the light-squared bishop and does nothing to stop Nb5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The signature move of the Jobava London against e6! The knight jumps to b5 threatening Nc7+ forking the king and rook. Black is forced to play the awkward Na6. Naroditsky shows that Black's piece on a6 will take several tempi to reroute, giving White a lasting initiative.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight jumps to b5 threatening Nc7+ fork, forcing Black's knight to the awkward a6 square, costing tempi.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with d4, staking our claim in the center and preparing to build the Jobava London setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 stakes the center and prepares the Jobava London setup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors us in the center with d5, contesting the space directly.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black contests the center directly with d5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the knight to c3 early — the signature Jobava move that sets us apart from the classical London.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to c3 early — the Jobava signature, deviating from the classical London setup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight, the most natural response.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the kingside knight to f6, attacking e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We bring out our dark-squared bishop before playing e3, ensuring it gets active placement outside the pawn chain.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Dark-squared bishop develops outside the pawn chain before e3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black solidifies the center with e6, preparing to develop the kingside bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e6 solidifies the center and prepares to develop the kingside bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the key Jobava idea — we lunge with Nb5, threatening Nc7+ and immediately disrupting Black's coordination.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nb5 threatens Nc7+, disrupting Black's coordination.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black is forced into this awkward square with Na6, as it is the only way to defend c7.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Na6 is forced to defend c7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play e3 to solidify our center and prepare to develop our bishop to d3.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 solidifies the center and prepares to develop the bishop to d3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop and prepares to castle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the bishop and prepares to castle.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete our minor piece development with Nf3, keeping a flexible and harmonious setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 develops the knight, keeping a flexible and harmonious setup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, seeking safety.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside, seeking safety.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We place our bishop on d3, pointing aggressively at the kingside and preparing to castle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to d3 aims at the kingside and prepares to castle.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays c6 to support d5 and give the misplaced Na6 a route back via c7.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays c6 to support d5 and give the misplaced Na6 a route back via c7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We retreat the knight back to c3, having achieved our goal of forcing the awkward Na6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Retreating the knight to c3 forces Black's knight to the awkward a6 square.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black's knight finally reroutes to c7, completing the regrouping.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight reroutes to c7, completing the regrouping.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle kingside, bringing our king to safety and connecting our rooks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Castling kingside brings the king to safety and connects the rooks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares to develop the queenside bishop to b7.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the queenside bishop to b7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We strike in the center with e4 — this is our key break, leveraging our lead in development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e4 strike opens the center, leveraging White's development lead.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures, accepting the central trade.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black captures, accepting the central trade.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We recapture with the knight, centralizing it powerfully.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Recapturing with the knight centralizes it powerfully.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black activates the bishop to b7, but we have already achieved a dynamic, active position.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's bishop to b7 activates it, but White already has a dynamic, active position.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with d4, initiating the Jobava London system.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 opens the Jobava London, targeting rapid development and early pressure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes central space with d5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black contests the center with d5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play our signature Nc3, immediately putting the Nb5 threat on the table.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 threatens Nb5, attacking the c7 weakness.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6, attacking e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our bishop actively to f4 before closing in the pawn chain.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Develop bishop to f4 before closing the pawn chain.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black preemptively plays a6 to prevent our Nb5 idea. While this stops the immediate threat, it is a tempo spent on a purely prophylactic pawn move.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's a6 prevents Nb5 but costs a tempo on a purely defensive pawn move.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We simply continue our development with e3, untroubled by Black's sidestep.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 develops while ignoring Black's sidestep.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6 to develop the kingside and solidify the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays e6 to develop the kingside and solidify the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We place the bishop on d3, targeting the kingside. We are already a tempo ahead in meaningful development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to d3 targets the kingside, and White is a tempo ahead in development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black challenges our center with c5, the most ambitious try.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's c5 challenges the center — the most ambitious try.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We capture with dxc5, inviting Black to spend more time recapturing while we continue developing.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: dxc5 invites Black to waste time recapturing while White develops.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures the pawn with the bishop, restoring material balance.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black recaptures with the bishop, restoring material balance.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our final minor piece with Nf3, completing our setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 develops the final minor piece, completing the setup.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle as well, bringing our king to safety and connecting our rooks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Castle to safety and connect rooks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queenside knight.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the queenside knight to c6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play a3 to prevent Nb4 and prepare b4 to gain space on the queenside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: a3 prevents Nb4 and prepares b4 to gain queenside space.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black advances with b5, supporting the bishop and staking queenside space.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's b5 stakes queenside space and supports the bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We centralize the queen with Qe2, connecting our rooks and preparing e4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qe2 centralizes the queen, connects rooks, and prepares e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to b7. We have a harmonious position with a slight lead in development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Our signature Nc3, keeping multiple threats and options in play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 keeps multiple threats and options in play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6, attacking e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the bishop actively to f4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop develops actively to f4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black opts for the fianchetto setup with g6. This creates a subtle weakness on the kingside that we will exploit.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's g6 fianchetto weakens the kingside dark squares for White to exploit.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play e3, solidifying our center before launching our plan.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 solidifies the center before launching the plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is our key idea against the fianchetto — we immediately advance h4 to exploit the weakened kingside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Advance h4 immediately to exploit the weakened kingside against the fianchetto.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays h5 to stop our h5 push, but this hands us control of the g5 square permanently.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's h5 cedes permanent control of g5 to White.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the knight to f3, preparing to reroute it to the e5 outpost.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pins our knight with Bg4, trying to neutralize our activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black pins our knight on f3 with Bg4, neutralizing our activity.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Be2, offering to trade bishops and remove the pin.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We plant our knight on the powerful e5 outpost, dominating the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queenside knight, trying to challenge our Ne5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We reposition the queen to d2, eyeing h6 and preparing to castle queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black must take, but now the h-file is completely open and the king is exposed.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We recapture with the queen on h5 with tremendous attacking energy. Qxf7 and mate ideas are already real.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We plant the knight on the powerful e5 outpost, leveraging our IQP position for active piece play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight on e5 outpost leverages the IQP for active piece play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with d4, beginning the Jobava London.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 starts the Jobava London.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d5, contesting the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays d5, contesting the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Nc3, the Jobava hallmark.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 is the Jobava hallmark, preparing d4 and a quick kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete the Jobava triangle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Jobava triangle completed.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black challenges our center with c5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black challenges the center with c5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Instead of e3, we capture with dxc5! This forces Black to spend time recapturing while we develop rapidly.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: dxc5 forces Black to recapture, giving White a lead in development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6, preparing to recapture the pawn with the bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays e6 to recapture the pawn with the bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play e3, solidifying our structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 solidifies the structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures the pawn with the bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black recaptures the pawn with the bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop the knight to f3, completing our minor piece development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to f3 completes minor piece development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We place the bishop on d3, aiming at the kingside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop on d3 aims at the kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queenside knight.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the queenside knight to c6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle kingside, bringing our king to safety.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Castle kingside to bring the king to safety.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays a6, preparing b5 for queenside expansion.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black plays a6 to prepare b5 for queenside expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play a3, preventing Nb4 and preparing b4 to contest the queenside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: a3 prevents Nb4 and prepares b4 to contest the queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black advances b5, gaining queenside space.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's b5 gains queenside space.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We centralize the queen on e2, connecting rooks and eyeing both flanks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen centralizes on e2, connecting rooks and eyeing both flanks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to b7.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the bishop to b7, fianchettoing on the queenside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We bring the rook to d1, centralizing on the open file.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Rook to d1 centralizes on the open file.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black centralizes the queen. We have achieved a harmonious position with superior development and easy play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's centralized queen faces White's superior development and harmonious position.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with d4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 opens the center and frees the queen and c1-bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 challenges the center and opens lines for the queen and c8-bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Nc3, setting up the Jobava.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 sets up the Jobava London.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6 or f6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Bf4, completing the setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 completes the setup, developing the bishop and eyeing c7.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e6 — but this allows our key tactical idea.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e6 allows our key tactical idea.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We jump to b5, threatening Nc7+ forking king and rook!
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: b5 threatens Nc7+ forking king and rook.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black tries to chase the knight with c6, but it's too late...
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's c6 comes too late — the knight is already entrenched.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The devastating fork! Our knight lands on c7 with check, attacking both the king and the rook on a8. This is the trap that punishes Black for ignoring the Nb5 threat.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight forks king and rook on c7 with check, punishing Black for ignoring the Nb5 threat.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black is forced to take with the queen, losing the right to castle.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black must recapture with the queen, forfeiting castling rights.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We recapture with the bishop, winning material and leaving Black's position in shambles.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Recapturing with the bishop wins material and leaves Black's position in shambles.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with d4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 opens lines for the queen and bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 challenges the center and opens lines for the queen and bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Nc3.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 develops the knight and defends e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play Bf4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bf4 develops the bishop, eyes the c7 pawn, and pressures Black's kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black fianchettoes — setting up for our kingside attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's fianchetto sets up for White's kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We play e3, preparing our assault.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 prepares the assault.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the bishop, eyeing the long diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We launch the h-pawn forward aggressively.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Push the h-pawn forward to open lines against the enemy king.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles into our attack — a critical mistake.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles into our attack — a critical mistake.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We sacrifice the h-pawn to rip open the kingside!
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Sacrificing the h-pawn rips open the kingside for a mating attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes the bait.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black takes the bait.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We sacrifice the rook! The h-file is now wide open.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Sacrifice the rook to open the h-file for mate.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures, but the king is completely exposed.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's capture exposes the king, leaving it vulnerable to attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The queen arrives with devastating threats. Qxf7 and mating ideas are everywhere.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qxf7 threatens mate, with the queen delivering devastating threats.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black tries to block with the bishop.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black blocks with the bishop, but it hangs to the pawn push.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop with tempo, preparing to bring more pieces into the attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Developing with tempo brings more pieces into the attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We point the bishop at the kingside, adding to the pressure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop aims at the kingside, increasing the pressure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black desperately tries to block diagonal lines.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black blocks diagonal lines to stop the queen's attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight jumps to g5, threatening Qxh7# and other mating patterns.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to g5 threatens Qxh7# and other mating patterns.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black tries to defend, but there is no escape.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's defense fails — no escape from the threat.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Checkmate! The queen delivers the final blow on h7. This is the ultimate punishment for castling into the h4-h5 attack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Queen mates on h7 — the punishment for castling into the h4-h5 attack.
